nativeRange.habitat: Generally found in sheltered, sandy or slightly muddy bottoms, often among seagrass meadows and on open stretches of coral sand from intertidal flats down to shallow reef areas.

decorPreferences: open sandy or rubble bottom for crawling
live rock arranged with stable bases (the star will push against rockwork)
minimal sessile invertebrate cover (no soft corals, clams, or tubeworms in the same tank)

diet.notes: The Chocolate Chip Sea Star is an opportunistic carnivore and scavenger. In the wild it has been documented preying on sessile invertebrates (animals that are permanently attached to the seafloor) including soft corals, sponges, tubeworms, clams, and other sea stars, as well as taking gastropods, sea urchins, and detritus. In captivity it is generally accepted to take chopped clams, shrimp, mussel, squid, and other fresh or frozen marine flesh placed near the star; some keepers also report success with sinking marine-invertebrate pellets. Spot-feeding every 2–3 days is commonly recommended.

goodWith: peaceful large saltwater fish (tangs, peaceful wrasses, dottybacks)
non-edible cleanup crew on a bare or rocky substrate
avoidWith: all corals (soft corals, LPS, SPS, zoanthids, mushrooms)
tridacnid clams and other sessile bivalves
other ornamental sea stars and small invertebrates it can overpower
obligate starfish predators (Harlequin Shrimp, Hymenocera picta)
starfish-nipping fish (triggerfish, large puffers, some large angelfish)

summary: A large, hardy Indo-Pacific sea star marked with chocolate-brown tubercles that resemble chocolate chips on a cookie. Best kept in mature fish-only (FOWLR) saltwater systems — it is NOT reef safe and will eat corals, clams, tubeworms, and small invertebrates.
careNotes: Among the more forgiving ornamental sea stars in the trade — generally regarded as hardier than Linckia or Fromia species — but is decisively NOT reef safe. Adults have been documented eating soft corals, sponges, tubeworms, tridacnid clams, other sea stars, and a variety of small sessile invertebrates, so this species should be reserved for fish-only or fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) tanks where coral and clam losses are not a concern. The trade overlap with the closely related Red Knob Sea Star (Protoreaster linckii, sw-echi-008) is significant: both are sometimes sold under shared common names such as 'Horned' or 'Knobbed Sea Star', and shipments are occasionally mixed; confirm tubercle color (chocolate brown vs bright red) at purchase. Like most echinoderms, this species is highly sensitive to copper and to rapid changes in salinity, pH, or temperature; long drip acclimation (60+ minutes) and a stable, well-cycled aquarium of several months' maturity are commonly advised. Never expose the animal to air during transfer — trapped air in the water-vascular system has been reported to cause serious tissue damage.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is not practical in home aquaria. Like most sea stars, Protoreaster nodosus has separate sexes (males and females are distinct individuals) and reproduces by releasing eggs and sperm into the water column, where fertilization occurs. The resulting larvae drift as plankton before settling. Hobby-scale larval rearing is not documented.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Primary source — confirmed scientific name Protoreaster nodosus, family Oreasteridae, Indo-Pacific origin, temperature 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.023-1.025, dKH 8-12, adult size up to 15" (~38 cm), care level 'easy', carnivore diet, peaceful temperament, NOT reef compatible (adults eat soft corals, sponges, tubeworms, clams, other starfish), copper-sensitive, requires drip acclimation, never expose to air.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Protoreaster nodosus)
additional.notes: Taxonomy authority — confirmed family Oreasteridae, order Valvatida, class Asteroidea; distribution restricted to western Pacific (Thailand to Samoa, Japan to New Caledonia); habitat sheltered sandy/muddy bottoms and seagrass meadows; adult size up to ~30 cm; opportunistic carnivore preying on sessile invertebrates including hard corals, sponges, gastropods, and sea urchins. Substituted for the designated Reef2Reef wiki secondary, which returned HTTP 403.

additional.name: Reeflings species guide
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed temperature 24-28°C, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.023-1.026, adult size ~30 cm, minimum tank 200 L (~53 gal), care level 'moderate to difficult', not reef safe, native to Philippines/Indonesia/northern Australia, requires sandy substrate and stable mature tank. Only source that supplied a numeric tank-size recommendation.

additional.name: Saltwaterfish.com care review
additional.notes: Cross-confirmed temperature 72-78°F, pH 8.1-8.4, SG 1.023-1.025, adult size up to 15", care level 'moderate' / very easy to acclimate, lifespan 5-10 years in captivity, peaceful slow-moving with some nocturnal activity, omnivorous scavenger consuming chopped clams/squid plus detritus, not reef compatible.
